title: “Justice, Are You Still There?”
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, numeral, name, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: A report appeared in the Deccan Chronicle dated 13 April 2015.

Under the headline “Karnataka Chief Justice to move to Orissa,” it said: “In a surprise move the Chief Justice of Karnataka High Court Dhirendra Hiralal Waghela has been shifted as Chief Justice of Orissa High Court..... Highly placed sources told Deccan Chronicle that, soon after the Supreme Court collegium of Judges headed by the Chief Justice of India H.L. Dattu recommended the transfer, Justice Waghela is understood to have expressed certain reservations in accepting it on the ground that the Orissa High Court is a smaller High Court compared to Karnataka High Court.”

Only a few newspapers, including Deccan Chronicle, Dinakaran and The Hindu Tamil, reported the transfer of the Karnataka High Court Chief Justice. Dinakaran wrote that Waghela took office as Chief Justice in March 2013; that, while Jayalalithaa's disproportionate-assets case was before the Bengaluru Special Court, he stood firmly behind trial judge Cunha and helped bring the case to its conclusion; that an order transferring him to Odisha was issued in the second week of March; that K.L. Manjunath was appointed in his place; that Waghela resumed the Chief Justice's duties after a week; and that he had now again been transferred to Odisha. It added that lawyers suspected powerful political intervention behind the transfer.
Although the transfer has occurred only now, our Murasoli published a boxed report on 29 December 2014 asking:
“Are these reports circulating in court circles true?

* Is it true that, because the Karnataka High Court Chief Justice is acting honestly in the Jayalalithaa disproportionate-assets appeal, differences have arisen with him and he is soon to be transferred?
* Is it true that there is a desire to transfer Chief Justice Waghela and place Justice Manjunath, next in seniority, in additional charge?
* Is it true, as is being said, that someone on behalf of the Central government is pursuing this?”
Has the Karnataka High Court Chief Justice not now been transferred in a manner that confirms what was then reported as talk in court circles?
Before you ask what his sudden transfer to a smaller High Court has to do with us, let me give you some details about Waghela.

On 2 February 2015, when the Karnataka High Court considered whether Bhavani Singh could continue as prosecutor in this case, Chief Justice Waghela said that under Section 24(1) of the CrPC, when a criminal case is transferred from one state to another, the government of the state to which it is transferred must appoint the Public Prosecutor.

He further told Bhavani Singh: “Your appearance in this appeal is contrary to law. Read Section 24(1). The Tamil Nadu government has no power to appoint the prosecutor in this case. Since the Karnataka government did not appoint the prosecutor, Bhavani Singh's appearance is unlawful.”

When the Chief Justice expressed these legal objections to Bhavani Singh's continuation, Sebastian, appearing for Singh, asked that Waghela not hear the case and that it be moved to another Bench. Waghela himself then said another Bench could hear it.

To be more explicit, at the very beginning Waghela asked Sebastian whether he objected to the Chief Justice hearing the case. Only after Sebastian said he had no objection did the hearing proceed. He objected only after Waghela made observations based on the rules. I recount this so that you may understand who Karnataka Chief Justice Waghela is.

There is more. Before Bhavani Singh, B.V. Acharya served as Special Public Prosecutor in the Bengaluru trial. He wrote an English account of his life titled All from Memory. Chapter twenty-five is titled “Case Against Selvi J. Jayalalitha, Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu.” On pages 206 to 220 he described his experiences as prosecutor and the errors and irregularities he said occurred in the trial. Who released the book? Chief Justice Waghela himself, at an event in Bengaluru on 15 November 2014. Justice Venugopal and former Union Law Minister Veerappa Moily also attended. Is it not relevant to recall that background now?

Around the time of Waghela's transfer, Raju, State Coordinator of the People's Rights Protection Centre, spoke to reporters about the Supreme Court granting Jayalalithaa bail a few days earlier. He alleged that Chief Justice Dattu had acted contrary to legal convention in granting bail to a former Chief Minister sentenced to four years in prison; that ordinarily the opposing party would be heard even in a routine bail matter, but neither Karnataka nor Tamil Nadu's Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption was heard; that the Supreme Court itself had held that reasons must be clearly stated when granting bail in corruption appeals, yet the order gave no reason for setting aside the Karnataka High Court's decision and ran to only four lines; and that the High Court had already explained in a forty-page order why bail should be denied to the four convicted persons.

Raju further said that although the four had petitioned only for bail, Chief Justice Dattu ordered the appeal itself to be expedited without their asking for it. With hundreds of cases pending in the Karnataka High Court, he called this “super” special treatment irregular and a danger signal to the public. Members of his organisation, he said, had petitioned the President with the signatures of one thousand lawyers seeking action against Chief Justice Dattu.

Before Jayalalithaa's bail plea came up in the Supreme Court, social activist Traffic Ramasamy complained that Fali Nariman's appearance for her violated Bar Council norms because Nariman's son was a Supreme Court judge. It is also worth remembering that Traffic Ramasamy petitioned Chief Justice Dattu himself to withdraw from the hearing because a corruption complaint concerning the case had been made against him.

Karnataka Chief Justice Waghela has now been transferred as Chief Justice of the Odisha High Court. The collegium headed by Chief Justice of India H.L. Dattu issued the order on Sunday night, the twelfth.

Everyone expects our esteemed judiciary to act quickly in deciding pending cases. But such speed in effecting “transfers” causes surprise. As a citizen concerned for this country, I seek answers to some reasonable questions from the guardians of law and justice.

When the office of Chief Justice of the Odisha High Court had been vacant since February 2015, why was this “transfer” suddenly made on a Sunday? When notification of the National Judicial Appointments Commission was to be issued on Monday, why was the transfer made the day before?

Why was the order made before the five-judge Constitution Bench was due on 15 April 2015 to hear the challenge to the National Judicial Appointments Commission, created to bring transparency to judicial appointments?

Nor can we forget that the transfer came before the Supreme Court's ruling on the validity of the Special Public Prosecutor's appointment in the disproportionate-assets appeal, and before the Karnataka High Court's judgment in that appeal. Can anyone be faulted for having doubts about this transfer?

If the Supreme Court's judgment on the fifteenth were to hold Bhavani Singh's appointment invalid, the Karnataka High Court Chief Justice would have to appoint a new judge to hear the appeal. The responsibility for appointing another prosecutor in Bhavani Singh's place would also rest with that Chief Justice. Since Justice Kumaraswamy, now hearing Jayalalithaa's appeal, was due to retire in August, the Chief Justice would also have to appoint the judge who would take over this important case. It is in this situation that he has been transferred.
Who is the primary cause behind all this high-level corruption? Why this transfer? I leave all of it to your inference.

We hold the independence, integrity and impartiality of the judiciary in the highest regard. At a time when the demand for transparent administration is growing throughout the country and every governmental decision is examined, what answer can we give when senior lawyers, impartial observers and journalists ask why the judiciary's administrative decisions alone should stand on a separate plane, beyond scrutiny?

In our democracy the judiciary stands above all and watches over every organ of democracy. Is it good for our country, for the democracy we cherish and protect, and for the principle we celebrate as “Truth Alone Triumphs” (Satyameva Jayate), that this lifeline of democracy should now face criticism on a scale never seen before?
With affection,
M.K.
15 April 2015
